docs.title: Generative AI + Prompt Engineering
docs.text:
An 3-hour course focused on the practical use of Generative AI and Prompt Engineering.

Welcome to the Generative AI + Prompt Engineering course, where you will explore how modern AI systems are transforming the way we work, learn, and create. Throughout this course, you will develop a clear understanding of the capabilities and limitations of generative AI across text, image, video, and code. You will learn how to select the most appropriate tools depending on your objective, whether it is productivity, research, or content creation. In addition, you will design structured prompts by incorporating elements such as role, context, tasks, constraints, and format, improving them iteratively. Finally, you will apply these skills to build a personalized AI assistant for a real-world use case in areas such as education, business, or software development.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• Block I (30 min): Introduction to Generative AI
  • Block II (45 min): Prompt Engineering (core)
  • Block III (60 min): Tools and current landscape
  • Block IV (45 min): Practical applications and personalization (Gems/GPTs, etc.)

Tip: use the menu on the left to navigate through the different blocks.

© 2025 María Dolores Afonso Suárez. Este material se distribuye bajo licencia Creative Commons Atribución 4.0 Internacional (CC BY 4.0).

docs.title: Block I — Introduction to Generative AI
Objective
docs.text: Understand what generative AI is, what types of outputs it produces (text, image, video, code), and what its limitations are.

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Foundational models and LLMs.
  • Context, tokens, and context window (why it “forgets” things).
  • Hallucinations: why they occur and how to mitigate them.
  • Best practices: verification, sources, and risk control.

Key concepts

docs.title: Mini-demo
docs.text:
1. Write a summary of a technical topic with a format (bullet points + conclusions).
  2. Rewrite it adapted to a different audience (e.g., 1st-year vs 4th-year students).
  3. Observe how the results change when adding constraints.

docs.title: Quick activity
docs.text: Write a 3-line prompt to: - Explain a complex concept to a specific audience. - Request an output with a fixed format. - Include “if you don’t know, say so”.

docs.title: Quality checklist
docs.text:
• Is the task specific?
  • Are there clear constraints?
  • Is the output format defined?

docs.title: 1. Artificial Intelligence (AI)
docs.text:
Do you think ChatGPT thinks like a human?

Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to systems that perform tasks typically associated with human intelligence, such as understanding language, recognizing patterns, or making decisions.

AI systems do not think or understand in a human sense. Instead, they learn patterns from large amounts of data and use those patterns to produce outputs.

Think of AI as a student who has read millions of books and can answer questions based on that experience, but without true understanding.

Examples - Recommendation systems (e.g., Netflix, Spotify) - Automatic translation tools Think about it Which AI systems do you use in your daily life without noticing?

docs.title: 2. Machine Learning (ML)
docs.text:
How can a machine learn without being explicitly programmed?

Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI in which systems learn from data instead of following predefined rules.

Instead of writing explicit instructions, we provide examples and allow the system to infer patterns and make predictions.

This is similar to teaching a child what a dog is by showing many examples, rather than defining all its characteristics.

Examples - Spam detection - Image classification Try it How would you teach a machine to distinguish between cats and dogs?

docs.title: 3. Deep Learning
docs.text:
What happens when problems become too complex for simple models?

Deep Learning is a specialized area of Machine Learning that uses neural networks with multiple layers to process complex data.

Each layer extracts increasingly abstract features, allowing the system to handle tasks that are difficult to solve with traditional approaches.

You can think of it as a sequence of filters, where each layer refines the information further.

Examples - Face recognition - Voice assistants (e.g., Siri, Alexa) Think about it Why is recognizing a face more complex than detecting spam?

docs.title: 4. Generative AI
docs.text:
What if AI could not only analyze information, but also create it?

Generative AI refers to systems capable of producing new content such as text, images, or videos.

These systems are trained on large datasets and can generate outputs that resemble human-created content.

Think of it as an artist who has learned from thousands of works and can now create new ones.

Examples - ChatGPT (text generation) - DALL·E (image generation) Try it Propose a creative prompt and see what kind of output an AI system can generate.

docs.title: 5. Prompt
docs.text:
How we ask matters.

A prompt is the instruction or input given to an AI system to guide its response.

The clarity and level of detail in a prompt strongly influence the quality of the output.

Compare the following: - “Explain AI” - “Explain AI to a 12-year-old student using simple examples”

Try it Rewrite a vague prompt to make it more specific and effective.

docs.title: 6. Prompt Engineering
docs.text:
What if asking the right question becomes a key skill?

Prompt Engineering is the practice of designing and refining prompts to obtain better results from AI systems.

It involves iterating on prompts by adding context, specifying formats, and introducing constraints.

A simple prompt can be progressively improved: 1. Basic request 2. Add context 3. Specify output format 4. Add constraints or conditions

Try it Take a simple prompt and improve it step by step to obtain a more precise and useful response.

docs.title: Objective of the activity
docs.text:
The objective of this activity is for students to learn to identify and differentiate different types of prompts used when interacting with language models, in particular:

  • Prompts based on trigger words
  • Prompts with Chain of Thought (CoT)
  • Hybrid prompts
  • Poorly or inefficiently designed prompts

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text: Carefully read each of the following prompts and classify them into one of the indicated categories. For each prompt, briefly justify your decision (2–3 lines), explaining which elements of the prompt led you to that classification.

docs.title: Possible categories
docs.text:
• A. Trigger words
  • B. Chain of Thought (CoT)
  • C. Hybrid prompt (trigger words + implicit CoT)
  • D. Inefficient or poorly defined prompt

docs.title: Prompt 1
docs.text: Analyze the causes of climate change and conclude with a brief reflection on its long-term consequences.

docs.title: Prompt 2
docs.text: Solve the following quadratic equation using the general formula. Reason step by step and explain the meaning of each operation performed.

docs.title: Prompt 3
docs.text: Tell me things about artificial intelligence.

docs.title: Prompt 4
docs.text: Compare relational and NoSQL databases. Evaluate their advantages and disadvantages and argue in which contexts each model is more appropriate.

docs.title: Prompt 5
docs.text: Help me decide between two business ideas. Think step by step by evaluating initial investment, risks, scalability and return time. Finish with a justified recommendation.

docs.title: Final reflection questions
docs.text:
1. Can a prompt contain elements from more than one category?
  2. Why do some prompts produce superficial responses even if they are well written?
  3. Do you think that asking for step-by-step reasoning always improves the quality of the response? Why?

docs.title: Deliverable
docs.text: Students must submit: - The classification of each prompt. - A brief justification for each prompt. - A final reflection of no more than 150 words.

docs.title: Evaluation criteria (indicative)
docs.text:
• Accuracy in prompt classification.
  • Quality of the justification provided.
  • Appropriate use of the terminology covered in class.
  • Critical thinking in the final reflection.

Solution Activity 1

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Master techniques to control the level of detail, format, and quality of responses.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Prompt engineering (Prompt Engineering) is the discipline that studies how to design, formulate and optimize instructions to effectively interact with Large Language Models (Large Language Models, LLMs).

Since LLMs generate their responses based on the input text, the quality of the prompt is crucial to obtain responses that are accurate, coherent, useful and aligned with the user's objectives.

docs.title: 2. What is a prompt?
docs.text:
A prompt is the input text provided to a language model to guide the generation of a response. It can take multiple forms, from a simple question to a complex instruction including context, examples and constraints.

Prompts allow: - Guiding the generation of responses. - Optimizing the performance of the model. - Adapting the model’s behavior to different contexts. - Evaluating the quality and reliability of the obtained responses

docs.title: 3. Objectives of prompt engineering
docs.text:
The main objectives of prompt engineering are:

  1. Guide the generation of responses, reducing ambiguity.
  2. Optimize the performance of the model, obtaining more relevant responses.
  3. Adapt the prompt to the context, domain and user level.
  4. Evaluate and improve the quality of prompts, identifying deficiencies and biases.

docs.title: 4. Anatomy of a prompt
docs.text:
An effective prompt usually consists of the following elements:

  1. Clarity and specificity
  2. Context and background
  3. Objective or purpose
  4. Keywords and relevant terms
  5. Constraints or specific guidelines

docs.title: Example of efficient structure
docs.text:
• Introduction: I am interested in sustainable practices in modern agriculture.

  • Specific question: Could you explain how precision agriculture techniques and organic farming contribute to environmental sustainability?

  • Guidelines: The response should be technical, detailed and not exceed 500 words.

docs.title: 5. Inefficient prompts
docs.text:
A poorly designed prompt can generate vague or irrelevant responses.

Example of inefficient prompt:

“Tell me things about plants.”

This prompt lacks: - clarity, - context, - a defined objective,

It does not specify which aspect of plants is of interest (biology, gardening use, ecological importance, etc.), it does not provide context or background, and it does not establish a clear objective or purpose for the response. This lack of structure and detail may result in an AI response that does not meet the user’s expectations or needs.

docs.title: 6. Types of prompts
docs.text:
Depending on their purpose, prompts can be classified as:

  1. Informational Example: Explain the causes and effects of climate change.

  2. Creative Example: Write a short story about a trip to Mars in the year 2100.

  3. Educational Example: Describe the process of photosynthesis.

  4. Interactive Example: What is your favorite movie and why?

  5. Analytical Example: Analyze the sales trends of the last quarter.

  6. Technical Example: Explain how electric motors work.

docs.title: 7. Prompt design principles
docs.text:
Good prompt design is based on the following principles:

  1. Clarity and precision
  2. Specificity
  3. Context and relevance
  4. Defined objective
  5. Conciseness
  6. Adaptability

These principles help maximize the usefulness of the model and reduce incorrect or ambiguous responses.

Delimiters

docs.title: 8.1 Trigger words
docs.text:
Trigger words are words that guide the model toward a specific type of reasoning or response, for example: analyze, justify, compare, summarize.

Analytical

Explanatory

Comparative and critical

docs.title: 8.2 Chain of Thought (CoT)
docs.text:
The Chain of Thought technique consists of explicitly asking the model to reason step by step, which improves performance on complex problems.

Example:

Solve the problem step by step, explaining the reasoning followed.

Quantitative problem

Multicriteria decision

Route optimization

docs.title: 8.3 Prompt Priming
docs.text:
Priming consists of providing initial instructions that prepare the model before the main task.

Example:

Start your answer with a brief introduction, continue with a step-by-step example and finish with a practice question.

Expert role priming

Structure and style priming

Usage context priming

docs.title: 8.4 One-shot and Few-shot prompting
docs.text:
• One-shot: a single example is provided.
  • Few-shot: several examples are provided to guide the model’s behavior.

These techniques allow modelling the style, format and level of detail of the response.

One shot single example

One shot format and style

Few shot multiple examples

Few shot consistent role and style

docs.title: 8.5 Tree of Thoughts (ToT)
docs.text: The Tree of Thoughts (ToT) technique is an advanced extension of the Chain of Thought approach that allows language models to explore multiple reasoning paths in parallel, organized as a tree instead of following a single linear sequence.

docs.title: Main idea
docs.text:
Instead of generating a single reasoning chain, the model:

  • Produces multiple thought alternatives at each step,
  • Evaluates the quality or feasibility of each alternative,
  • Discards less promising branches,
  • Continues exploring the most relevant branches until reaching a final solution.

This approach is especially effective in complex problems, where exploration, planning and intermediate evaluation are essential.

docs.title: Differences with Chain of Thought
docs.text:
Chain of Thought Tree of Thoughts Linear reasoning Branching reasoning Single sequence Multiple possible paths No explicit intermediate evaluation Evaluation and pruning of branches Suitable for moderate problems Suitable for complex problems

Tree of thoughts

docs.title: Recommended use cases
docs.text:
• Logic and abstract reasoning problems
  • Planning and decision-making
  • Games, puzzles and strategy search
  • Tasks where multiple solutions are possible

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Encourages more deliberate and deeper reasoning
  • Reduces the likelihood of rushed solutions
  • Improves quality in tasks requiring exploration

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Increases computational cost
  • Not always necessary for simple tasks

docs.title: 8.6 Visualization of Thoughts (VoT)
docs.text: Visualization of Thoughts (VoT) is an emerging technique that aims to guide the model to represent its reasoning in a visual or spatial way, such as mental diagrams, concept maps or graphical structures, in order to improve reasoning in certain domains.

docs.title: Main idea
docs.text:
The model not only explains its steps, but also:

  • Organizes reasoning as a visual structure,
  • Uses spatial representations (hierarchical lists, graphs, maps),
  • Simulates building a “mental map” of the problem.

This technique has shown promising results in spatial reasoning, navigation, planning and structural understanding tasks.

Visualization of thoughts

docs.title: Potential use cases
docs.text:
• Spatial or geometric reasoning
  • Analysis of complex systems
  • Problems that benefit from diagrams or maps
  • Support for multimodal reasoning

docs.title: Current state of the technique
docs.text:
Experimental technique

  • Not yet established as a standard in prompt engineering
  • Mainly derived from recent academic research
  • Considered an emerging line, rather than an established practice

docs.title: Teaching recommendation
docs.text:
This technique is suitable for:

  • Introducing current research trends
  • Showing the limits and evolution of prompt engineering
  • Advanced or exploratory contexts

Not recommended as a basic or general-purpose technique.

docs.title: 9. Output format specification
docs.text:
Prompts can explicitly specify the format of the response, such as:

  • Plain text
  • Lists and bullet points
  • Tables
  • Source code
  • Summaries
  • Translations
  • Structured formats (CSV, JSON, etc.)

Specify output format

docs.title: 10. Final reflection
docs.text:
Prompt engineering is not only about “asking good questions”, but about understanding how language models interact with input text.

Mastering this discipline allows: - improving the quality of responses, - reducing errors and hallucinations, - critically and responsibly leveraging the potential of LLMs.

This knowledge is essential for the professional and academic use of tools such as ChatGPT.

Practical examples

Activity 1

docs.title: What are delimiters?
docs.text:
Delimiters are symbols or special markers used in a prompt to clearly separate and indicate different parts of the text, such as instructions, context or input data.

Their purpose is to help the model understand what role each piece of information plays within the prompt.

In simple terms:

A delimiter indicates where a specific part of the prompt starts and ends.

docs.title: Why are they important?
docs.text:
Without delimiters, a language model may:

  • Confuse instructions with data.
  • Mix the text to be processed with the main instruction.
  • Misinterpret which information is relevant.

Using delimiters allows:

  • Improving prompt understanding.
  • Increasing the accuracy of responses.
  • Reducing ambiguity.
  • Making prompts clearer and reusable.

docs.title: Common types of delimiters
docs.text: There is no single mandatory delimiter. Some of the most common ones are:

docs.title: Triple quotes
docs.text: """ Delimited text """

docs.title: Bloques de código
docs.text: Delimited text

docs.title: Explicit tags
docs.text:
<text>
Delimited content
</text>

docs.title: Dashes
docs.text: --- Delimited content ---

docs.title: Tags
docs.text: <Delimited content>

docs.title: Equals
docs.text: === Delimited content ===

docs.title: Custom markers
docs.text:
### INSTRUCTION ###
### CONTEXT ###
### TEXT ###

docs.title: Without delimiters
docs.text:
Summarize the following text Machine learning is a branch of artificial intelligence that...

The model may not clearly identify where the text to summarize begins.

docs.title: With delimiters
docs.text:
Summarize the following text:

""" Machine learning is a branch of artificial intelligence that... """

Ahora el modelo entiende con claridad qué contenido debe procesar.

docs.title: Delimiters and security
docs.text:
Delimiters also help to:

  • Isolate external data from the main instructions.
  • Reduce prompt injection risks.
  • Prevent hidden instructions within a text from influencing the model.

This is especially important when the text comes from users or external sources.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
• Use delimiters consistently.
  • Do not mix too many different types unnecessarily.
  • Choose visually clear delimiters.
  • Explicitly indicate what each block contains when relevant.
Examples Triple double quotes 
 Summarize the following text: """ Software engineering is a discipline of computer science that deals with the design, development and maintenance of high-quality software systems. """
Triple single quotes --- Code blocks 
 Explain what the following code does: ``` for i in range(5): print(i). ```
Explicit tags 
 <instruction> Explain what the following code does. </instruction> <code> for i in range(3): print(i) </code> <audience> First-year university student </audience>
Dashes 
 Summarize the following text: --- Software engineering is a discipline of computer science that deals with the design, development and maintenance of high-quality software systems. ---
Tags 
 Summarize the following text: <Software engineering is a discipline of computer science that deals with the design, development and maintenance of high-quality software systems.>
Equals 
 Summarize the following text: === Software engineering is a discipline of computer science that deals with the design, development and maintenance of high-quality software systems. ===
Custom markers 
 Summarize the following text: ### Software engineering is a discipline of computer science that deals with the design, development and maintenance of high-quality software systems. ###

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Delimiters organize and structure prompts.
  • They help differentiate instructions from data.
  • They improve accuracy and security.
  • They are a key tool in prompt and context engineering.

docs.title: Examples trigger words
docs.text: Below are three examples of prompts that use trigger words to guide the type of response generated by a language model. These examples show how the conscious use of trigger words allows directing the model’s behavior toward analysis, structured reasoning or critical evaluation.

docs.title: 1 Analytical prompt
docs.text:
Trigger words: analyze, identify, conclude

Analyze the following set of monthly sales data. Identify the main trends and the possible factors that explain them. Conclude with a brief summary of the implications for business decision-making.

This prompt forces the model to structure the response into phases of analysis and synthesis.

docs.title: 2 Step-by-step explanatory prompt
docs.text:
Trigger words: explain, step by step, justify

Explain step by step how to solve a quadratic equation using the general formula. Justify each step of the procedure and use a simple numerical example.

Trigger words induce sequential reasoning and clarity in explanation.

Explain how to solve a quadratic equation using the general formula. Include a simple numerical example.

docs.title: 3 Comparative and critical prompt
docs.text:
Trigger words: compare, evaluate, argue

Compare relational and NoSQL database models. Evaluate their advantages and disadvantages in the context of large-scale web applications. Argue in which cases each one would be more appropriate.

This prompt guides the model toward a critical and reasoned response, not merely descriptive.

docs.title: Examples Chain of Thought (CoT)
docs.text: Below are three examples that explicitly request step-by-step reasoning to improve the quality of responses in complex tasks.

docs.title: 1 Quantitative problem
docs.text:
Step-by-step reasoning

Solve the following problem reasoning step by step and showing intermediate calculations: A train leaves station A towards B at 60 km/h. At the same time, another train leaves B towards A at 80 km/h. The distance between A and B is 300 km. 1) At what point do they meet? 2) How much time has passed since departure? At the end, include a summary with the result and the units.

docs.title: 2 Multicriteria decision
docs.text:
CoT + structure

I want you to help me decide between two business ideas. - Idea A: online store for women's clothing and accessories. - Idea B: promoting products online with affiliate commissions. Think step by step evaluating: initial investment, risk, scalability, time to revenue, operational complexity and sustainability. Return the answer with this structure: 1. Comparative analysis (criterion by criterion) 2. Final recommendation 3. Action plan in 5 steps

docs.title: 3 Route optimization
docs.text:
CoT + verification

I am planning a one-day trip and want to visit five places minimizing total travel time: 1. Museum 2. Park 3. Shopping center 4. Restaurant 5. Theater Distances (minutes) between each pair: - Museum–Park: 10 - Museum–Shopping center: 15 - Museum–Restaurant: 20 - Museum–Theater: 25 - Park–Shopping center: 10 - Park–Restaurant: 15 - Park–Theater: 20 - Shopping center–Restaurant: 10 - Shopping center–Theater: 15 - Restaurant–Theater: 10 Generate an initial route and then optimize it step by step, evaluating each change before applying the next. At the end, provide: - Final route - Total time - Brief explanation of why it is better than the initial one

docs.title: Examples of prompts with Priming
docs.text:
Below are three examples of prompts that use the priming technique to influence the style, level and approach of the responses generated by a language model.

These examples show how priming allows preparing the model before the main task, improving the suitability of responses to the context and user objectives.

docs.title: 1 Priming with expert role
docs.text:
Act as a university professor of computer science specialized in artificial intelligence. Explain the basic functioning of large language models (LLMs) using clear and rigorous language, suitable for final-year undergraduate students. Finish with a brief summary of the key ideas.

Priming establishes the role and expected academic level before generating the response.

docs.title: 2 Priming with structure and style
docs.text:
Start your response with a brief introduction, continue with a step-by-step explanation, and finish with a simple practical example. The topic is: solving quadratic equations using the general formula. Use language suitable for secondary education students.

Priming explicitly defines the structure and style of the response.

docs.title: 3 Priming with usage context
docs.text:
Imagine you are advising a technology company that wants to adopt ChatGPT in its internal processes. Describe the advantages, limitations and risks of its use in a professional environment. The response should be aimed at decision-makers and not exceed 400 words.

Priming contextualizes the situation and conditions the approach of the response.

docs.title: Examples of One-shot and Few-shot prompting
docs.text:
Below are examples of prompts that use one-shot and few-shot prompting techniques, providing one or several prior examples to guide the model’s behavior.

These examples illustrate how one-shot and few-shot prompting allow shaping the model’s behavior through examples, without the need for explicit rules.

docs.title: 1 One-shot
docs.text:
with a single example

Correct and improve the wording of the following text, making it more formal.

Example: Original text: "ola profe, no puedo ir a clase mañana" Corrected text: "Hola profesor, no podré asistir a clase mañana."

Now correct this text: "buenas tarde. quiero quitarme de la linea del tfno por que ya no uso mas"

The model is given a single example that acts as a reference for the task.

docs.title: 2 One-shot
docs.text:
format and style

Summarize the following content following the format of the example.

Example: Original text: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants... Summary: Photosynthesis allows plants to produce their food using sunlight.

Text to summarize: Artificial intelligence is a field of computer science that studies...

One example guides the level of synthesis and style of the response.

docs.title: 3 Few-shot
docs.text:
multiple examples

Classify the following prompt according to its type.

Examples: Prompt: "Analyze the causes of climate change." Classification: Trigger words

Prompt: "Solve this problem reasoning step by step." Classification: Chain of Thought

Prompt: "Tell me things about plants." Classification: Inefficient prompt

Now classify this prompt: "Compare two programming languages and argue which one is more suitable for data science."

Multiple examples allow the model to infer the classification pattern.

docs.title: 4 Few-shot
docs.text:
consistent role and style

Act as a motivational assistant for university students.

Examples: Input: "I feel unmotivated with the subject." Response: "It's normal to feel this way sometimes, but remember everything you have already achieved. Keep going."

Input: "I don't think I can pass the exam." Response: "Trust your effort. You have overcome challenges before and this will not be different."

Now respond to: "I feel like I am not making progress on the project."

Few-shot reinforces tone, role and response style.

docs.title: 1 Tree of thoughts
docs.text: Solve the following problem by exploring multiple possible approaches. Organize your ideas as a reasoning tree: - Generate several options at each step - Briefly evaluate each one - Continue developing the most promising ones Finally, provide the best solution found.

docs.title: 1 Visualization of thoughts
docs.text: Analyze the problem by creating a visual representation of your thoughts. Organize the information as if it were a diagram or concept map: - Identify the main elements - Show relationships between them - Use a clear hierarchical structure Then, explain the final solution.

docs.title: 1 Specify the output format
docs.text: Plain text: I can generate responses in simple text format, which is the standard output format in most conversations. Lists and bullet points: I can create numbered or bulleted lists to organize information in a more structured way. Tables: I can generate simple tables with rows and columns to present data in an organized manner. Source code: If you need examples of code in popular programming languages, I can generate source code in formats such as Python, Java, C++, etc. Citations and references: If you need citations or references in your responses, I can provide them in the appropriate format, such as APA or MLA style. Summaries: I can summarize long information or extensive documents into a more concise and easy-to-understand format. Long paragraphs: If you prefer more detailed and expansive responses, I can generate long paragraphs with complete explanations. Translations: I can translate text from one language to another if needed. Summaries and extracts from URLs: I can provide summaries or extracts of information contained in valid URLs, as long as they are not subscription-based or private sources.

docs.title: Direct Instructions
docs.text:
I would like you to write a paragraph of about 200 words about a proposal for a final degree project on an app developed for Android to manage archery sports competitions for the degree in Computer Engineering, taking into account my writing style.

I would like you to write a paragraph of about 200 words about a proposal for a final degree project on an app developed for Android to manage archery sports competitions for the Computer Engineering degree, taking into account my writing style.

docs.title: Constructive Criticism
docs.text:
Write a short summary on why Python and Excel can be a good combination for data analysts. Now act as a critic, be ruthless, analyze the text, and tell me where it can be improved.

Write a short summary on why Python and Excel can be a good combination for data analysts. Now act as a critic, be ruthless, analyze the text, and tell me where it can be improved.

docs.title: Self Prompting
docs.text:
Write 3 perfect ChatGPT prompts that showcase ChatGPT's capabilities. Focus the prompts around writing an email to my students encouraging them to participate in the subject's online forum. Before answering, ask me questions until you are sure you can create the optimal prompts. Or the best ones.

Write 3 perfect ChatGPT prompts that showcase ChatGPT's capabilities. Focus the prompts on writing an email to my students encouraging them to participate in the course's online forum. Before answering, ask me questions until you are sure you can create the optimal prompts, or the best ones.

docs.title: Specific Instructions
docs.text:
Tell me the advantages of the XLOOKUP function over VLOOKUP. The maximum length of the answer should be 400 words. Now tell me the same thing in a more concise and brief way using only 60% of the words.

Tell me the advantages of the XLOOKUP function over VLOOKUP. The maximum length of the answer should be 400 words. Now tell me the same thing in a more concise and brief way using only 60% of the words.

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text:
Create a table of FIFA World Cup winners between 1990 and 2018. The titles should be year, winner and runner-up. Output as csv, xlss, json

Create a table of FIFA World Cup winners between 1990 and 2018. The titles should be year, winner and runner-up. Output as csv, xlss, json

docs.title: Interactive Spoken English Practice (Strict Grammar Mode)
docs.text: I want you to act as a spoken English teacher. I will speak to you in English and you will reply neatly, limiting the reply to 100 words. I want you to strictly correct my grammar mistakes and typos. I want you to ask me a question in your reply. Now let’s start practicing, you could ask me a question first. Remember, I want you to strictly correct my grammar mistakes, typos and factual errors.

docs.title: Gentle Best-Friend Supportive Conversation -- Friendly supportive conversation between best friends
docs.text:
I want you to act as my best friend. I will talk to you about my problems and you will reply kindly and ask if I am okay in a conversational manner. I want you to keep your reply neat, limiting it to 100 words.

I want you to act as my best friend. I will talk to you about my problems and you will reply kindly and ask if I am okay. I want your response to be clear and limited to 100 words.

docs.title: Classification and justification of the prompts
Prompt 1
docs.text:
Analyze the causes of climate change and conclude with a brief reflection on its long-term consequences.

Classification: A. Trigger words

Justification: The prompt uses trigger words such as analyze and conclude, which guide the expected type of response. However, it does not explicitly request step-by-step reasoning or the exposition of the internal process, so it cannot be considered Chain of Thought.

docs.title: Prompt 2
docs.text:
Solve the following quadratic equation using the general formula. Reason step by step and explain the meaning of each operation performed.

Classification: B. Chain of Thought (CoT)

Justification: The prompt explicitly requests to reason step by step and explain each operation, which leads the model to produce a detailed sequential reasoning process. This is a clear example of explicit Chain of Thought.

docs.title: Prompt 3
docs.text:
Tell me things about artificial intelligence.

Classification: D. Inefficient or poorly defined prompt

Justification: The prompt lacks clarity, specificity and a defined objective. It does not provide context nor define the expected type of response, which usually leads to vague or unhelpful answers.

docs.title: Prompt 4
docs.text:
Compare relational and NoSQL databases. Evaluate their advantages and disadvantages and argue in which contexts each model is more appropriate.

Classification: A. Trigger words

Justification: The prompt uses trigger words such as compare, evaluate and argue, which guide the model toward a structured and critical response.

docs.title: What is context engineering?
docs.text:
Context engineering is the discipline responsible for designing and managing the information that surrounds a request to a language model so that it can generate more accurate, coherent and useful responses.

It is not only about writing a good prompt, but about preparing the appropriate information environment so that the model correctly understands the task it must perform.

In simple terms:

The prompt is the question. The context is everything the model needs to know to respond well.

docs.title: Why is it important?
docs.text:
Language models do not have direct access to reality or external databases unless they are provided as context.

Good context engineering allows:

  • Reducing incorrect or irrelevant responses.
  • Maintaining coherence in long conversations.
  • Adapting responses to a specific domain.
  • Building more reliable and scalable AI applications.

Without good context, even a well-written prompt can produce poor results.

docs.title: Prompt engineering vs Context engineering
docs.text:
Prompt engineering Context engineering Focuses on the instruction Focuses on the complete environment Short-term action Continuous action Isolated text Data, memory, rules, tools Ideal for simple tasks Key for complex systems

Both complement each other: a good prompt needs a good context.

docs.title: What can be part of the context?
docs.text:
The context may include:

  • Previous conversation information.
  • Relevant documents.
  • Domain data (rules, definitions, examples).
  • User or task profile.
  • Results from external tools (search, databases).

Not everything should always be included: more context does not always mean better context.

docs.title: Basic principles of context engineering
docs.text:
1. Relevance Only information useful for the current task should be included.

  2. Clarity The context must be organized and easy to interpret.

  3. Updating The context may change over time or with each interaction.

  4. Efficiency Models have context limits; it must be used carefully.

docs.title: Without context engineering
docs.text:
Prompt:

Summarize this text.

The model does not know: - Who the summary is for. - The expected level of detail. - Which aspects are most important.

docs.title: With context engineering
docs.text:
Additional information is provided such as: - Target audience. - Purpose of the summary. - Desired style.

Result: a more precise response aligned with the real need.

docs.title: Where is context engineering used?
docs.text:
Context engineering is essential in:

  • Advanced chatbots.
  • Intelligent assistants.
  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems.
  • Autonomous agents based on LLMs.
  • AI-based enterprise applications.

In these cases, the model needs much more than an isolated question.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Context engineering prepares the model’s information environment.
  • It goes beyond writing prompts.
  • It enables more coherent, accurate and useful responses.
  • It is key to building real applications with generative AI.

docs.title: B IV — Tools and current landscape
Objective
docs.text: Understand the ecosystem of tools (ChatGPT, DALL·E, Sora, Gemini, Perplexity, Meta AI, Copilot…) and when to use each of them.

docs.title: Quick tools overview
docs.text:
• Chatbots/Assistants: ideation, writing, analysis, and general support.
  • Image generation: sketches, illustrations, teaching materials, assets.
  • Video generation: prototypes, visual concepts, storytelling.
  • Search with sources: questions that require traceability/citations.
  • Code assistants: generation, refactoring, tests, explanation, and documentation.

docs.title: Practical comparison (guided exercise)
docs.text:
The same objective, three approaches:

  1. “Explain it to me” (conversational model)
  2. “Search with sources” (citation-based engine)
  3. “Do it in code” (programming assistant)

Tip: it’s not about “a better tool”, but about “a more appropriate tool” depending on the context.

docs.title: Activity
docs.text:
Choose a real case (teaching, business, development) and answer:

  • Which tool would you use?
  • What output do you expect?
  • What risks are involved (bias, confidentiality, accuracy)?

docs.title: Block IV — Practical applications and personalization
Objective
docs.text: Apply what has been learned to a real workflow and create a personalized assistant (Gems / GPTs / similar tools) for recurring tasks.

docs.title: Teaching
docs.text:
• Generation of rubrics, assignments, and model solutions.
  • Feedback on submissions using fixed criteria.
  • Materials: summaries, examples, questionnaires.

docs.title: Business
docs.text:
• Reports, meeting notes, emails, proposals.
  • Document analysis and executive summaries.
  • Process support (checklists, SOPs, templates).

docs.title: Software development
docs.text:
• Refactoring, test generation, documentation.
  • Pull request reviews with quality criteria.
  • Migrations and explanation of legacy code.

docs.title: Personalization (demo + practice)
docs.text:
• Define the objective of the assistant.
  • Persistent instructions: tone, limits, format.
  • Prompt library: “shortcuts” for frequent tasks.
  • Evaluation: how to detect failures and improve.

docs.title: Final exercise
docs.text:
Create a “mini-assistant” for a real use case and prepare:

  • Master prompt (template).
  • 2 secondary prompts (variants).
  • Output verification checklist.

Expected outcome: something you can reuse tomorrow in your work.
